findings.description: fetch_events runs an unbounded query (no LIMIT, line 38) and calls .fetchall(), materializing the entire events-by-users join into a Python list; for a wide date range this is an unbounded OOM/latency risk on the report path (render_activity_report).

rows += f"..." concatenates the HTML body string inside a per-event loop, an O(n^2)-worst-case string-building anti-pattern; accumulate into a list and "".join() (or use io.StringIO / a generator) for O(n).

render_activity_report holds three full copies of the dataset in memory at once -- the events list, the concatenated rows string, and the final html string -- roughly tripling peak memory; streaming row rendering instead of buffering all rows would cut this.
_connect opens and closes a brand-new SQLite connection on every public-method call with no connection reuse/pooling, adding per-request connection-setup overhead in a web-service context.

Range scan + JOIN + GROUP BY (lines 36-39, 67-69) depend on indexes on events.created_at, events.user_id and users.id; if those columns are unindexed these become full table scans -- schema is out of scope so this is speculative.
DB-sourced display_name/action/created_at are interpolated directly into HTML with no escaping, enabling stored XSS; flagged briefly -- defer detail to critic-security.

Query uses created_at < end (exclusive upper bound) but the docstring on line 32 claims both boundary days are included, an off-by-one correctness mismatch; flagged briefly -- defer detail to critic-logic.
